# A social habit?

Health of the mind and body are the primary reasons for we *should* want to stop — but then, they always have been. We don't actually need scientific research and knowledge in neuroscience to tell us short-form content is addictive and can potentially shatter our lives. These bodies of ours are the most sophisticated objects on the planet, and any user knows from the first session that the stimulus can go to excess and turn poisonous.

The only reason why we ever get involved with short-form content is the cycle's overlap with our evolutionary programming. Short-form content is highly available, free and streaming twenty-four hours a day. Short-form content was once considered harmless, but that was when the images were static and the videos involved a trip to the local store for a VHS tape.

Today, it's generally considered — even by users themselves — that short-form content is a supernormal stimulus and addiction-forming. In the old days, the heavy scroller didn't admit that the feed controlled them. In every pub, club or bar, many people would proudly compare memes, clips, trends, and screen time. Today, the position is beginning to reverse for the internet short-form addict. Today's user realises they're beginning to feel they don't need the offline world. Banding together online, they discuss experiences, devise strategies and explore options. Today's strong user doesn't want to depend on drugs. Through social revolution, all users are giving serious thought to stopping short-form content and scrolling. Today's users consider short-form content a useless and harmful activity.

The most significant trend noticed on forums is the increasing emphasis on the anti-social aspects of short-form content; the days when someone boasted about constant scrolling are slowly being replaced with realisation of slavery to the short-form content monster.

The only reason why people continue after being educated is because they've failed to stop or are too frightened to try. There's a wide spectrum of interest in the subject, some abstaining from short-form content, scrolling and dopamine hit — with or without partners. Practices that separate presence from reward-seeking — such as dopamine fasting, app blockers, and deliberate long-form attention — are discussed and adopted in droves. Many aforementioned failures are in reality fall-forwards, thus somewhat benefiting people practising them. Once you start the no-short-form route you'll find the best fit that applies to your life; it's encouraged to devise your own plan on dopamine hits after understanding and practising attention separation. Whatever your route, you'll see value in limiting the number of times you flush your brain with chemicals through dopamine hit, and never again seeing short-form content and dopamine hits as a pleasure or crutch for your emotional self.

Various popular online communities founded by non-users are dedicated to quitting not only short-form content but also scrolling. These sites are ultimately beneficial to those escaping, but most notes point them to try willpower. The consequence of obsession with abstinence streaks and other measures is self-pity and lack of elation. Much of the brainwashing remains alive and well. Eventually, someone breaks down and a domino effect takes place, other users finding out they aren't the only ones. However, their efforts aren't in vain, they're falling forward, albeit with lots of self-torturing as they shut down their browsers but not the desire and need. EasyPeasy works in the reverse, shutting down the need and desire first, before shutting down the browser. Every day more and more users leave the sinking ship and those left on it become terrified they'll be the last.

**DON'T LET IT BE YOU!**
